messages.content:
Proofread this email and improve the quality :Dear Rebecca,

Two of my robots have arrived, and if needed, I would be happy to send them to your place. Here is information about these two robots:

Project Title: 線驅可鎖定機器人，Author: Botao Lin, Year: 2022
Project Title: 單臂同心管機器人，Author: Yi Lu, Year: 2016

Additionally, I have uploaded two videos. The first video is the video for JingDongX robotic challenge, and the second video is for human-robot cooperate assembling. You can get access to these two videos through the following URL:
1.https://drive.google.com/
2.https://drive.google.com/

If you have any problems (i.e. cannot open the videos) regarding these videos and/or robots, please feel free to contact me.

Best regards,
<PRESIDIO_ANONYMIZED_PERSON>

Dear Rebecca,

I wanted to let you know that I have received two of my robots, and I am open to send them to you if you need them. Here is some information about the two robots:

1. Project Title: 線驅可鎖定機器人; Author: Botao Lin; Year: 2022.
2. Project Title: 單臂同心管機器人; Author: Yi Lu; Year: 2016.

Furthermore, I have uploaded two videos for you. The first video shows the JingDongX robotic challenge, and the second video displays the human-robot cooperate assembling. You can access both of these videos by clicking on the following URLs:

1. https://drive.google.com/
2. https://drive.google.com/

If you encounter any issues while trying to open the videos or have any other concerns regarding these robots, please do not hesitate to contact me.

Best wishes,

<PRESIDIO_ANONYMIZED_PERSON>
Is the quoted expression right? "Here is the requested information about these two robots"

The quoted expression "Here is the requested information about these two robots" is grammatically correct, but it sounds a bit formal and may not be necessary depending on the context of the email. An alternative could be "Here is some information about these two robots" or "I have information about these two robots that you requested."
Upcomming
The correct spelling is "upcoming."
Thanks
You're welcome!
Hi, what is the correct translation of "有針對性的問題" in English?
The correct translation of "有針對性的問題" in English is "targeted questions" or "specific questions."

Thanks, could you please help me to proofread and improve the quality of  the following email? Email: "Dear Prof. Amy,

Hello, I am <PRESIDIO_ANONYMIZED_PERSON>, a new lecturer at FCA.

According to Ben's email, we are going to host a MSC interview together at Thursday (April 20). 
Since this is my first time participating interview at MPU, could you please kindly tell me what I shall do during the interview?

p.s., I wonder whether we can get access to the materials of the candicates so that we can perpare some specific questions.

Best regards,
<PRESIDIO_ANONYMIZED_PERSON>"

Dear Prof. Amy,

I hope this email finds you well. My name is <PRESIDIO_ANONYMIZED_PERSON>, and I am a new lecturer at FCA.

I understand from Ben's email that we will be hosting an MSC interview together on Thursday, April 20th. As this is my first time participating in an interview at MPU, I would greatly appreciate it if you could advise me on what I should expect and do during the interview.

Also, I was wondering if it would be possible to gain access to the materials of the candidates beforehand so that we can prepare some targeted questions specifically for the interview.

Thank you for your time, and I look forward to working with you.

Best regards,

<PRESIDIO_ANONYMIZED_PERSON>
Thanks, it looks great. However could you please explain the usage of "we will be hosting"? It seems grammarly incorrect for me?

"We will be hosting" is the future continuous tense which is used to talk about an action that will happen in the future and will continue for a period of time. In this case, hosting refers to the act of conducting or facilitating an event or interview. 

In the context of the email, "we will be hosting" is correct since the interview has been scheduled for a specific date in the future and it implies an ongoing process during the interview. 

Please note that while Grammarly may flag it as incorrect, this phrase is grammatically correct and commonly used in business emails.

Amy replies : "Dear <PRESIDIO_ANONYMIZED_PERSON>,

Actually, I'm not available for next Thursday. I've already sent an email to Ben, and still waiting for his reply. Besides, Ben will send us the students list.

Best,
Amy" Please write a response letter for me.

Dear Prof. Amy,

Thank you for your response. I apologize for any inconvenience caused by my previous email. I appreciate you informing me that you will not be available for the interview on Thursday. I hope everything works out with Ben's schedule and we can find another suitable time to hold the interview.

Thank you for letting me know that we will be receiving the students' list from Ben. I will keep an eye out for that email.

Once again, thank you for your time, and I look forward to working with you in the future.

Best regards,

<PRESIDIO_ANONYMIZED_PERSON>

could you please help me to proofread and improve the quality of the following email? Email: "Dear Prof. Jacky,

The email is well recieved. I'll read the materials and discuss with you tomorrow.

Thank you and best regards,
<PRESIDIO_ANONYMIZED_PERSON>"

Dear Prof. Jacky,

I hope this email finds you well. I wanted to confirm that I have received your email, and I appreciate you sending me the materials. I will take the time to review them thoroughly before we meet tomorrow to discuss.

Thank you for taking the time to share these materials with me. I am excited to delve further into the subject matter.

Best regards,

<PRESIDIO_ANONYMIZED_PERSON>
The modified version is too formal. Please write if in a more casual way, thanks.

Sure, here's a more casual version:

Hey Professor Jacky!

Thanks for your email, I just wanted to let you know I've received the materials and I'll take a look before we chat tomorrow. Looking forward to our discussion!

Thanks again,

<PRESIDIO_ANONYMIZED_PERSON>
What's wrong with "The email is well recieved"

There is nothing inherently wrong with the phrase "The email is well received," but it sounds a bit formal and old-fashioned. It would be more natural to say "I received your email" or "Thanks for your email" in a casual or modern context.
Thanks, but the expression of "take a look" is too casual in this email.

You're right, "take a look" is a bit informal, and it may not be suitable for some formal settings. Here is another suggestion that strikes a balance between being polite and professional while maintaining a casual tone:

Dear Professor Jacky,

Thank you for your email. I just wanted to let you know that I have received the materials, and I'll make sure to go over them before we meet tomorrow to discuss further.

Thank you for taking the time to send me these materials, and I am looking forward to our discussion tomorrow.

Best regards,

<PRESIDIO_ANONYMIZED_PERSON>
